title_en: Wolf packs don't have alpha males and alpha females

note:
根据牛津英语语料库的时间，英语中最常用的名词是。其频率部分是由于其多种含义，部分原因是其在常见短语中使用。最重要的是，“时间”是普遍存在的，因为它指的是指示人类生命的所有方面，从我们睡到睡觉的时间和大多数之间的一小时。
 但是是时候了？当然，讽刺是很难说。试图使用单词钉住它的含义可能会感觉像抓住鞭打鱼。 4世纪基督教神学家圣奥古斯丁很好地弥补了困境：
 但是，我们在说什么时我们会熟悉和明知时间？当我们谈论它时，我们肯定会理解;当我们听到另一个人的话时，我们也会理解。那么，是时候了吗？如果没有人问我，我知道;如果我想向他解释，我不知道。
 我们大多数人都是生活在一个时间只是一个时钟读取的世界里。跨学科艺术家Alicia Egger不是。通过共同的时钟和商业标牌的形式（广告牌，霓虹灯迹象，那种充气尼龙在停车场在汽车经销商的空中舞者中动画），Eggert使概念艺术邀请我们体验到时间的尺寸我们用来谈论的语言。
 她的艺​​术从物理和哲学的时间理论借鉴了，就像时间和空间的不可分割性以及存在的差异。她通过简单的单词和短语表达了这些文化思想，我们在日常生活中使用，从而使它们变形和可关联。
  拿“现在”和“那样”。在最狭隘的意义上，“现在”意味着这一刻。但它可以扩大到今天，今年，本世纪，et克朗塔。 “然后”可能意味着过去和未来。 Eggert的“现在然后”探索这两个时间关系如何彼此取决于彼此。 “现在”和“然后”单词是连接到相同空气源的尼龙的可充气雕塑。风扇是可逆的，所以一个词字面意识到另一个字。
  在时间的哲学中，那些归咎于永恒主义的人相信所有时间同样真实，而过去，现在和未来都存在同时存在。在“一直，”Eggert通过改变时钟给予这种哲学方法的材料形式，使其十二个运作的小时手。 
在费城的一家便利店的屋顶上是永久安装的“你所看到的所有光线”。霓虹灯在两个陈述之间交替：“你看到的所有灯都是从过去的”和“你所看到的只是过去。”它还完全关闭了短暂的时间。
 “这谈到了光线需要时间旅行的事实，所以当它到达你的眼睛时，你看到的一切都在技术上已经过去了，”eppert写在艺术品的描述中。 “从月球的光线留下了它的表面1.5秒前;阳光在触及皮肤之前达到8分8分钟和19秒。我们看起来更远的太空，我们可以看到的更远。“
 “我的工作有不同的水平，”Eggert告诉我。 “在表面级别，大多数人都非常容易获得和理解。但是，你可以剥离不同的层数，更深刻地思考它实际上的内容，有机会问那些大的存在问题或者有那些反思，内省时刻。“
  Eggert住在德克萨斯州德克萨斯州丹顿，她是北德克萨斯大学雕塑和工作室艺术教授。她的工作已经在美国，欧洲和亚洲的文化机构展出。
  她对时间的兴趣和她的焦点，让她的艺术能够来自令人惊讶的来源：福音派基督教。
 “我在一个关于永恒，来世的所有想法以及我们在这里种植了我的所有这些想法，”她说。“ “那种你在教堂谈论的一切。”
 她出生于新泽西州卡姆登的一个宗教家庭，在01981年。她参加了教会 - 她的父亲是一位五旬节，每周两次，周三再一次。 
“在不同的宗教中，有不同程度的重要性，对人们是否真正理解或感受到圣经或消息感觉，”她说。 “与在服务期间讲拉丁语的天主教堂不同，在五旬节教堂，我长大的教会，目标是将人们带入折叠，并确保每个人都感受到欢迎并了解正在发生的事情：”我们正在谈论的事情大，疯狂，事情，但我们将把它放在这个包装中，你可以吞下来。“我认为这是某种方式嵌入在我身上。”
 虽然Eggert受到媒介的影响，但她更令人怀疑。在基督教世界观，她被教导，地球上的生活遭遇了。拯救仅在永恒之后来了。
 “那些真的是真的错了，”她说。 “我们有这个生命生活，态度是：'我不能等到它结束，所以我可以到达天堂。”
 “我开始思考，潜意识地，没有真正有意识地，关于”小现在“态度对世界的不利影响以及几乎所有的东西，在某种程度上，我可以看到更清楚的成年人，”她说。 “如果你觉得生活就是痛苦，那么死后就是当你在天上的时候，贯穿你的所有行为。它渗透到其他动物和物种的态度，以及物种：'所有这一切都只是临时，所以我们不一定要保存它。“
 由高中，Eggert知道她不再相信基督教。 （她最终会在大学的一名无神论者上“出来”给父母）。她开始越来越关注这个想法，就像她把它一样，“我们有时间是我们唯一的时间。”她开始阅读哲学并挑选了对佛教​​的“不是很严重”的兴趣，探讨这个世界观的含义：如果我们只有一个人生命，那么，那么，“时间”是什么意思？ “永恒”是什么意思？
  在她在德雷塞尔大学的最后一学期，她正在学习室内设计，她发现概念艺术提供了一个门户来探索这些问题。
 “突然间，我明白我可以制作由一个想法而不是形象的艺术，”她说。 “并且您可以将一个想法带入存在的许多不同的方式，从简单地将其与文本进行通信，以使用各种不同的雕塑材料，流程和形式。” 
在追求阿尔弗雷德大学的雕塑中的雕塑硕士学位的同时，她开始与她的艺术惯例及时合并她的哲学兴趣。
  在02008的早期作品上标题为“现在的长度”，茄子在水中浸泡了一根红色绳子，并将其冻结成“现在”这个词的形状。然后她挂在墙上并在融化时拍摄它。 （“现在，”在那艺术品中，结果为2分钟，四十三秒）。
  “咖啡杯输送带腰带日历”重新称达每天喝一杯咖啡的日常仪式，每天早上都是时间，每个杯子都代表一天。瓷杯慢慢地在传送带上慢慢地走过它的帖子 - 它的笔记“明天”，“明天”，“之后的第二天，”之后的第二天。“每二十四小时，一个杯子掉下了传送带的末端，打碎成一堆。在堆上破碎的杯子上面是标有“昨天”的帖子。因为杯子由未陶瓷制成，所以碎片可以被重建成滑块并重塑进入循环的杯子。
 “这是我第一次做出了一种动力学雕塑，将时间和运动进入工作，”Eggert说。 “也是我第一次真正开始考虑周期性与线性时间。这让我开始阅读时间，因为它在物理学中的思考。“
  Eggert认为“现在之间”，然后是一个突破性的一块。这是她的第一个试验标牌，可以在她的工作室外面的走廊墙上找到。在标志的一边是“现在”这个词。另一，这个词“那么”。走路的人可以轻易误认为是浴室标志。 Eggert看到它是“通过人的道路，分割时间和空间切片的刀片”。
 “那是我真的开始主要按时拍摄工作的时候，并给予语言身体形式，”Eggert说。 “一会儿，我无法弄清楚这两种事情是如何相关的，但现在似乎是我主要做的结合。”
 一个时钟告诉你它是什么时候。 Eggert的艺术问你什么时候。她不提供这么多的答案，这是一种可能性的星座。 
“我对人们思考时间的不同方式着迷，”她说。 “有人说它存在。有人说它不存在。有人说现在的一切都是，其他人说离散的时刻都像书的各页一样叠加，这就是我们那个时间是线性的。没有办法证明任何关于时间的思考方式都是正确的。可能从所有不同的解释都有很小的位和部分，这可能形成答案。我不知道。我只是想知道人们尽可能多的解释。“
 在02015年，她遇到了一种思考与她深深共鸣的时间。它会激发两项艺术品，包括光明雕塑，“现在的时刻”，最近被史密森尼的瑞克画廊，总理博物馆和装饰艺术博物馆收购。
   在01999中，禅宗诗人加里斯奈德派出了斯图尔特品牌，环保主义者和网络化先锋的北方人，以创造和编辑全地目录：
  斯奈德的诗歌暗示着现在的过去。品牌回应了他自己的侵略，关于未来如何存在：
  当时，品牌在工作中完成了漫长的时钟，这是一本散文的论文，将读者引入了10,000年时钟的想法和长期的基础，他共同创立了Brian Eno和Danny Hillis的非营利组织在01996。
 这本书是一个明朗的呼吁，以便参与长期思维，并考虑长期责任来平衡文明的“病理短暂的关注跨度”。品牌认为，通过扩大我们的“现在”的感觉，包括过去10,000年（“迄今为止的文明规模”）和未来10,000年，人类可以超越短期思维并与目前的挑战结合在一起渴望的思想。这座20,000年的参考框架现在是漫长的，现在是Brian Eno创造的术语。
  品牌包括他与斯奈德的交流，因为这本书更接近。 （斯奈德最终会在今后的那一刻在02016的诗集中的贡献中包括他的贡献。 
品牌的EPIGRAM成为充满了“引用报价”的书中最具共享的选择之一，经常出现在激励推文和主题演讲的幻灯片中。当报价归因于独自归因于他时，他总是在信贷斯奈德中。我最近问品牌是否只是给予信贷的信用，或者如果他觉得在孤立时观看的两首诗之间存在更深的联系。 （另一个着名的品牌Quip，“信息想要自由，”经常在没有至关重要的第二部分的情况下分享：“信息也希望昂贵。”）
 “信用和连接都有助于报价，”品牌告诉我。 “Gary将Gravitas带来了信誉。这也是他开始的序列。两次进一步的书籍结束时间，向后和向前，现在长的方式试图。“
 “这是一个谈话，”品牌说。 “几乎所有艺术都是，并不总是如此公开。”
 在02018中，Eggert将她的声音添加到谈话中。她已经读了现在三年来漫长的时间，并发现了长时间的概念，这是一个引人注目的纠正小于现在她在宗教中看到的不利影响。她特别受到品牌的“现在的那一刻......”的表演。
 “我正在寻求一个不断的文学来阅读，这将是我对时间的理解的另一个明星，”她说。 “当我正在阅读这些书籍时，我作为艺术家的过程，我总是在附近保留素描。如果有一个引用跳出来，我写下了。然后，当我在对展览的新工作进行头脑风暴时，我会常常通过我的笔记本返回，看看我写下的所有报价。我不知道如何或为什么在那个特定的时间选择品牌的话语，但我开始意识到他们准确地说我在那一刻是真实的或重要的感觉。“
 当Eggert在Twitter上询问品牌时，她是否可以有权将报价转为霓虹灯雕塑，他的回答是典型的：“当然！” （品牌的个人网站的页脚阅读：“请不要要求借用我的东西：只是做到这一点”）。
  “现在的时刻”，最初在葡萄牙的葡萄牙嘉兰多桑托斯在02019年在葡萄牙的Galeria Fernando Santos展出，并于02022年在史密森尼的瑞克画廊亮相，是一个霓虹粉红色的标志，这是十二英尺高，十五英尺宽。它通过两个陈述循环。首先：“这个现在/时刻/曾经是难以想象的/未来。”然后，在几秒钟之后，“现在”的词语和“难以想象的”眨眼，离开：“这个/时刻/曾经是/未来。”然后标志关闭了。在一个节拍后，循环重复。 
“现在的时刻”不仅仅是品牌的epigram作为标志。通过增加观众如何体验到消息的时间元素，Eggert使品牌的单词立即，动态和个人。
  乍一看，这件作品传达了一个粗暴的简单真理：从过去的角度来看，这一刻 - 就像你读过我的话一样，并采取这个标志的动画 - 曾经是未来的。但随着时间的推移，出现的问题：片刻多久了？它是标志之间的间隔，然后再打开吗？什么时候“现在”结束？什么时候“未来”开始？
 当然，这一切都取决于你的观点。如果您的下次会议开始时，将来可能是十秒秒下一季度，如果你是商人;下次选举，如果你是政治家;一个幻想，如果你是佛教徒。如果你强调，如果你受到压力，或者显然无穷无尽，你的感觉可能是不可能的。这一切都取决于你愿意考虑的东西，以及你愿意注意的是什么。
 关注“现在的时刻”的注意时间越长，它就越有意义。
 “你可以显然放大这个时刻，因为现在就是这样，”Eggert告诉我。 “或者你可以缩小这个时刻更长时间。标志闪烁的方式是提醒这两者的提醒。当它完全关闭几秒钟后，然后开始循环循环，你现在提醒真的很短。但实际的单词也表明了更长时间。“
 也许，盯着那个标志，你开始意识到这两个关于时间的陈述总是真实的，对于任何冥想它们的人来说总是如此。他们是你的祖先。他们现在的时刻是什么样的？他们会为你的后代真实。什么难以想象的未来等待他们？
 “在我们的日常生活中，我们倾向于简短地思考，并将目前的时刻视为小而狭隘，”Eggert说。 “但是，在过去数百万年的脚下形成岩石的同样的自然法则仍然有效，现在正在进行中。看起来好像我们的集体未来可能取决于我们想象一个“目前的时刻”的能力更长，更广泛 - 我们有限的视野无法包含。“ 
这种心理时间旅行是同理心的运动。 “现在的时刻”的力量，就像埃克特的艺术品一样，这是它的简单，并且可以易于访问，使运动感觉像看着时钟一样直观。
  Eggert的独奏展，“可能性条件”，在德克萨斯州达拉斯的Liliana Bloch Gallery在02021年4月开始。大多数画廊空间被她最新的艺术品占据了“难以想象的未来”。
  一个伴侣“现在的时刻”，“难以想象的未来”由六层钢钢筋组成，“未来”一词占据了负面空间。安装在附近的墙壁上是三个小型动力学结构，使用时钟手拼写“现在”单词以不同的速度。现在漫长的（由钢铁“未来”雕塑代表）和现在的短片（由三个动力学“现在代表现在”雕塑）在同一时刻共存。
  展览反映了Eppert的信念，当我们与探索语言和时间的艺术互动时 - 她所谓的“强大但无形的力量，塑造了我们现实” - 我们可能不仅仅是关于什么是真实的，而且可能是有可能的。
  “艺术为我们提供了深入和有意义地思考个人和社会的机会，”埃克特说。 “艺术为我们提供了新的方式，讲述了同样的故事 - 持续更引人注目的方式，更感情，更可关联和更具体验。这些经历创造了了解世界的新方法以及我们在其中发挥的角色。艺术是想象其他不可想象的期货的可能性。“
  在02021年5月，现在谈话，肖恩卡罗尔，一位物理学家，着作时的作品强烈影响埃克特的工作，谈到这种能力如何想象难以想象的期货是使人类生活有意义的。
 作为一个例子，他指着巴塞罗那的Sagrada Familia，由Antoni Gaudi设计的现代主义大教堂。建设始于01883年，而Gaudi没有幻想，它将在他死亡时期完成，这在01926年发生。它仍然在这一天建设。 
“这一点不是高迪认为他会随着时间的推移是一种幽灵般的持久性，这将俯视大教堂并欣赏它，”卡罗尔说。 “他在未来前景的那一刻就越来越高兴。这是我们人类有能力的东西。现在，我们自我的条件取决于我们过去和未来的愿景，以及我们在现在的条件。“
  我们是宇宙中的临时少许的复杂结构，这是随着时间的推移的一部分熵的整体增加。这意味着我们是短暂的。我们不会永远持续下去。这是坏消息。我们不会持续10到10年到10年。我们有一个寿命。我们有一个到期日。但它也意味着我们很有意思。我们是宇宙的有趣部分。这种复杂性的一部分是我们对自己和宇宙的思考能力和宇宙的其余部分，以做什么心理学家称之为心理时间旅行，以为自己不仅仅是在不同的地方，而且在不同的时代。这是这种能力，这种想象力，通过时间流动，这使我们成为人类的人。
 忘记我们有这种能力来想象难以想象的期货是非常容易的。这太容易忘记了时间可能意味着超过这里的狭隘问题 - 现在或者希望拯救永恒的永恒。
note_en:
The most commonly-used noun in the English language is, according to the Oxford English Corpus,  time. Its frequency is partly due to its multiplicity of meanings, and partly due to its use in common phrases. Above all, “time” is ubiquitous because what it refers to dictates all aspects of human life, from the hour we rise to the hour we sleep and most everything in between.
 But what is time? The irony, of course, is that it’s hard to say. Trying to pin down its meaning using words can oftentimes feel like grasping at a wriggling fish. The 4th century Christian theologian Saint Augustine sums up the dilemma well:
 But what in speaking do we refer to more familiarly and knowingly than time? And certainly we understand when we speak of it; we understand also when we hear it spoken of by another. What, then, is time? If no one asks me, I know; if I wish to explain to him who asks, I know not.
 Most of us are content to live in a world where time is simply what a clock reads. The interdisciplinary artist Alicia Eggert is not. Through co-opting clocks and forms of commercial signage (billboards, neon signs, inflatable nylon of the kind that animates the air dancers in the parking lots of auto dealerships), Eggert makes conceptual art that invites us to experience the dimensions of time through the language we use to talk about it.
 Her art draws on theories of time from physics and philosophy, like the inseparability of time and space and the difference between being and becoming. She expresses these oftentimes complex ideas through simple words and phrases we make use of in our everyday lives, thereby making them tangible and relatable.
  Take the words “now” and “then.” In its most narrow sense, “now” means this moment. But it can be broadened to refer to today, this year, this century, et cetera. “Then” can mean both the past and the future. Eggert’s “Between Now and Then” explores how these two time relationships depend on one another. The words “NOW” and “THEN” are inflatable sculptures of nylon connected to the same air source. The fan is reversible, so one word literally sucks the air out of the other.
  In the philosophy of time, those who ascribe to  eternalism believe all time is equally real, and that the past, present, and future all exist simultaneously. In “All the Time,” Eggert gives this philosophical approach material form by altering a clock to give it twelve functioning hour hands.
  On the roof of a convenience store in Philadelphia is the permanent installation “All the Light You See.” The neon sign alternates between two statements: “All the light you see is from the past” and “All you see is past.” It also turns off completely for a brief time.
 “It speaks to the fact that light takes time to travel, so by the time it reaches your eyes, everything you are seeing is technically already in the past,” Eggert  writes in a description of the artwork. “Light from the moon left its surface 1.5 seconds ago; sunlight travels for 8 minutes and 19 seconds before it touches your skin. The farther out into space we look, the farther back in time we can see.”
 “There are different levels to my work,” Eggert tells me. “At the surface level, it’s extremely accessible and understandable by most people. But then you can peel away the different layers, think more deeply about what it is actually saying, and have the opportunity to ask those big existential questions or have those reflective, introspective moments.”
  Eggert lives in Denton, Texas, where she is professor of sculpture and studio art at the University of North Texas. Her work has been exhibited at cultural institutions throughout the United States, Europe, and Asia.
  Both her interest in time and her focus on making her art accessible come from a surprising source: evangelical Christianity.
 “I was raised in an environment where all these ideas about eternity, the afterlife, and why we’re here were planted in me from an early age,” she says. “That’s kind of all you talk about at church.”
 She was born into a religious family in Camden, New Jersey, in 01981. She attended church — where her father was a Pentecostal minister — twice every Sunday, and again on Wednesdays.
 “In different religions, there are different levels of importance placed on whether or not people actually understand or feel moved by the scripture or the message,” she says. “Unlike in a Catholic church that speaks Latin during the service, in the Pentecostal church I grew up in, the goal was to bring people into the fold and make sure everyone felt welcome and understood what was going on: ‘We’re talking about big, crazy, things, but we’re going to put it in this package that you can swallow.’ I think that was somehow embedded in me.”
 While Eggert was influenced by the medium, she was more skeptical of the message.In the Christian worldview she was taught, life on Earth was suffering. Deliverance came only in the eternal hereafter.
 “Something about that just kind of struck me as really wrong,” she says. “We have this one life to live, and the attitude was: ‘I can’t wait until it’s over, so I can get to heaven.’”
 “I started thinking, kind of subconsciously, not really consciously, about the detrimental effects that ‘small now’ attitude had on the world and almost everything, in a way I can see much more clearly as an adult,” she says. “If you feel like life is all about suffering, and then after death is when you get to be in heaven, that permeates all of your actions. It permeates the attitudes that we take towards the planet, to other animals, and species: ‘All of this is just temporary anyways, so we don’t necessarily have to conserve it.’”
 By high school, Eggert knew she no longer believed in Christianity. (She would eventually “come out” to her parents as an atheist in college). She started to focus more and more on the idea that, as she puts it, “the time we have is the only time we have.” She started reading philosophy and picked up a “not very serious” interest in Buddhism to explore the implications of that worldview: if we only have one life to live, what, then, does “time” mean? What does “eternity” mean?
  In her last semester at Drexel University, where she was studying interior design, she discovered that conceptual art offered a gateway to explore these questions.
 “All of a sudden, I understood that I could make art that was driven by an idea instead of an image,” she says. “And there are so many different ways you can bring an idea into existence, from just simply communicating it with text, to using all kinds of different sculptural materials, processes, and forms.”
 While pursuing a Master in Fine Arts in sculpture at Alfred University, she began to merge her philosophical interest in time with her artistic practice.
  In an early piece from 02008 titled “The Length of Now,” Eggert soaked a yarn of red string in water and froze it into the shape of the word “now.” She then hung it on a wall and filmed it while it melted. (“Now,” in that artwork, turned out to be two minutes and forty-three seconds long).
  “Coffee Cup Conveyor Belt Calendar” reimagined the daily ritual of having a cup of coffee on the way to work each morning as units of time, with each cup representing a day. The porcelain cups traveled slowly across a conveyor belt past post-it notes that read “Today,” “Tomorrow,” “The day after that,” “The day after that.” Every twenty-four hours, a cup fell off the end of the conveyor belt, shattering into a pile. Above the pile of shattered cups was a post-it note labeled “Yesterday.” Because the cups were made of unfired porcelain, the shards could be reconstituted into slip and remade into cups that started the cycle anew.
 “That was the first time I ever made a kinetic sculpture that incorporated time and movement into the work,” Eggert says. “It was also the first time that I really started to think about cyclical versus linear time. That led me to start reading about time as it’s thought about in physics.”
  Eggert considers “Between Now and Then” to be a breakthrough piece. It was her first experiment with signage, and could be found mounted on the wall of the hallway outside of her studio. On one side of the sign was the word “NOW.” On the other, the word “THEN.” Those who walked by could easily mistake it for a bathroom sign. Eggert saw it as “a blade that slices through a person’s path, dividing time and space.”
 “That was when I really started to focus primarily on time with my work, and giving language a physical form,” Eggert says. “For a little while, I couldn’t figure out how those two things were related, but now that seems to be the combination of what I do primarily.”
 A clock tells you what time it is. Eggert’s art asks you what time is. She doesn’t provide answers so much as present a constellation of possibilities.
   “I’m fascinated by all the different ways people think about time,” she says. “Some say it exists. Some say it doesn’t exist. Some say the present moment is all there is, others say discrete moments all stack up like the individual pages of a book, and that’s why we have this illusion that time is linear. There’s no way to prove that any of these ways of thinking about time is actually right. There’s probably little bits and pieces from all the different explanations that maybe form an answer. I don’t know. I just want to know as many of the explanations people have thought of as possible.”
 In 02015, she came across a way of thinking about time that deeply resonated with her. It would inspire two artworks, including a light sculpture, “This Present Moment,” that was  recently acquired by the Smithsonian’s Renwick Gallery, the premier museum of American craft and decorative arts.
   In01999, Gary Snyder, the Zen poet, sent an epigram to Stewart Brand, the environmentalist and cyberculture pioneer best known for founding and editing the  Whole Earth Catalog:
  Snyder’s poem alluded to how the present becomes past. Brand responded with a riff of his own, on how the future becomes present:
  At the time, Brand was at work completing  The Clock of the Long Now, a book of essays that introduced readers to the ideas behind the  10,000 Year Clock and  The Long Now Foundation, the nonprofit organization he co-founded with Brian Eno and Danny Hillis in 01996.
 The book was a clarion call for engaging in long-term thinking and taking long-term responsibility to counterbalance civilization’s “pathologically short attention span.” Brand argued that by enlarging our sense of “now” to include both the last 10,000 years (“the size of civilization thus far”) and the next 10,000 years, humanity could transcend short-term thinking and engage the challenges of the present moment with the long view in mind. This 20,000-year frame of reference is known as the Long Now, a term  coined by Brian Eno.
  Brand included his exchange with Snyder as the book’s closer. (Snyder would ultimately include his contribution in a 02016 collection of poetry,  This Present Moment).
 Brand’s epigram became  one of the most shared selections from a book full of  “quotable quotes,” regularly appearing in motivational tweets and the slides of keynote presentations. He always makes a point of crediting Snyder when the quote is attributed to him alone. I asked Brand recently whether this was simply a matter of giving credit where credit was due, or if he felt there was a deeper connection between the two poems that was lost when viewed in isolation. ( Another famous Brand quip, “Information wants to be free,” is often shared without the crucial second part: “Information also wants to be expensive.”)
 “Both credit and connection help the quote,” Brand told me. “Gary lends gravitas with the credit. Also it’s a sequence, which he started. The two riffs book-end time, backward and forward, the way Long Now tries to.”
 “It’s a conversation,” Brand said. “Nearly all art is, not always so overtly.”
 In 02018, Eggert added her voice to the conversation. She’d read  The Clock of The Long Now three years earlier, and found the concept of the Long Now a compelling corrective to the detrimental effects of the small now she saw in religion. She was particularly struck by Brand’s “This present moment…” epigram.
 “I’m on a constant hunt for literature to read that will be another star in that constellation of my understanding of time,” she says. “My process as an artist is, as I’m reading these books, I always keep a sketchbook nearby. If there’s ever a quote that jumps out at me, I write it down. Then, when I’m brainstorming for new work for an exhibition, I’ll oftentimes go back through my notebooks and look at all the quotes that I’ve written down. I don’t know how or why I ended up choosing Brand’s words at that particular time, but I started realizing that they were saying exactly what I was feeling at that moment to be true or important.”
 When Eggert  asked Brand on Twitter whether she could have permission to turn the quote into a neon sculpture, his response was typical: “Sure!” (The footer of Brand’s personal website reads: “Please don’t ask for permission to borrow my stuff: just do it”).
  “This Present Moment,” which initially went on display at the Galeria Fernando Santos in Porto, Portugal in 02019 and will debut at the Smithsonian’s Renwick Gallery in 02022, is a neon pink sign that is twelve feet tall and fifteen feet wide. It cycles through two statements. First: “This present / moment / used to be / the unimaginable / future.” Then, after a few seconds, the words “present” and “unimaginable” blink off, leaving: “This / moment / used to be / the / future.” And then the sign turns off. After a beat, the cycle repeats.
 “This Present Moment” is more than Brand’s epigram rendered as a sign. By adding the element of time to how viewers experience the message, Eggert has made Brand’s words immediate, dynamic and personal.
  At first glance, the piece conveys a deceptively simple truth: from the perspective of the past, this moment — as in, right now, as you read my words, and take in an animation of that sign — was once the future. But as time passes, questions arise: how long is a moment? Is it the interval between the sign turning off, and turning back on again? When does “now” end? When does “the future” begin?
 It all depends, of course, on your perspective. The future might be ten seconds from now if that’s when your next meeting starts; next quarter, if you’re a businessperson; next election, if you’re a politician; an illusion, if you’re a Buddhist. Your sense of now might be impossibly brief, if you’re stressed, or apparently endless, if you’re mindful. It all depends on what you’re willing to consider, and what you’re willing to pay attention to.
 The longer you pay attention to “This Present Moment,” the more meaningful it becomes.
 “You could obviously zoom in on this present moment as being right now,” Eggert tells me. “Or you could zoom out on this present moment as being much longer. The way the sign flashes is a reminder of both of those things. When it turns off completely for a couple of seconds, and then starts to cycle back through, you’re reminded of that really short now. But the actual words suggest a much longer now as well.”
 Perhaps, staring at that sign, you begin to realize that these two statements about time are always true for any human being who contemplates them. They were true for your ancestors. What was their present moment like? They’ll be true for your descendants. What unimaginable future awaits them?
 “In our everyday lives, we’re inclined to think in short terms and see the present moment as small and narrow,” Eggert says. “But the same laws of nature that formed the rocks beneath our feet millions of years in the past are still in effect and in progress right now. And it seems as though our collective future might depend on our capacity to conceive of a ‘present moment’ that is much longer and wider — one that our limited field of view cannot contain.”
 Such mental time travel is an exercise in empathy. The power of “This Present Moment,” like so much of Eggert’s artwork, is that it’s simple enough, and accessible enough, to make that exercise feel as intuitive as looking at a clock to check the time.
  Eggert’s solo exhibition, “Conditions of Possibility,” opened at the Liliana Bloch Gallery in Dallas, Texas, in April 02021. A majority of the gallery space is occupied by her latest artwork, “The Unimaginable Future.”
  A companion piece to “This Present Moment,” “The Unimaginable Future” consists of six layers of steel rebar with the word “FUTURE” occupying the negative space. Mounted on a nearby wall are three small kinetic structures that use clock hands to spell the word “NOW” at different speeds. The Long Now (represented by the steel “Future” sculpture) and the Short Now (represented by the three kinetic “Now” sculptures) coexist in the same moment.
  The exhibition reflects Eggert’s conviction that when we engage with art that explores language and time — what she calls “the powerful but invisible forces that shape our reality” — we might wonder not only about what is real, but what is possible.
  “Art provides us with opportunities to think deeply and meaningfully about what we value as individuals and as a society,” Eggert says. “Art gives us new ways of telling the same stories — ways that are continually more compelling, more emotive, more relatable and more experiential. Those experiences create new ways of understanding the world and the role we play in it. Art is a condition of possibility for imagining otherwise unimaginable futures.”
  In  a May 02021 Long Now talk, Sean Carroll, a physicist whose writings on time have strongly influenced Eggert’s work, spoke about how this capacity to imagine unimaginable futures is what makes human life meaningful.
 As an example, he pointed to Barcelona’s Sagrada Familia, a modernist cathedral designed by Antoni Gaudi. Construction began in 01883, and Gaudi harbored no illusions that it would be completed by the time of his death, which occurred in 01926. It remains under construction to this day.
 “The point is not that Gaudi thought that he would be a ghostly persistence over time that would be looking down on the cathedral and admiring it,” Carroll said. “He gained pleasure right at that moment from the prospect of the future. And that’s something that we humans have the ability to do. The conditions of our selves, right now, depend on our visions of the past and the future, as well as our conditions here in the present.”
  We are temporary little bits of complex structure in the universe that are part of the overall increase of entropy over time. That means we are ephemeral. We’re not going to last forever. That’s the bad news. We’re not going to last for 10 to the 10 to the 10 years. We have a lifespan. We have an expiration date. But it also means we are interesting. We are the interesting part of the universe. Part of this complexity is our ability to think about and model ourselves and the rest of the universe to do what psychologists call mental time travel, to imagine ourselves not just in different places, but at different times. It’s that ability, that imagination, that flow through time, that makes us what we are as human beings.
 It is all too easy to forget that we have this capacity to imagine unimaginable futures. It is all too easy to forget that time can mean more than the narrow concerns of the here-and-now or the hoped-for salvation of a timeless eternal then.
title: 现在有多长？

The U.S. Department of Justice is elevating investigations of ransomware attacks to a similar priority as terrorism in the wake of the Colonial Pipeline hack and mounting damage caused by cyber criminals, a senior department official told Reuters.
 Internal guidance sent on Thursday to U.S. attorney's offices across the country said information about ransomware investigations in the field should be centrally coordinated with a recently created task force in Washington.
 "It's a specialized process to ensure we track all ransomware cases regardless of where it may be referred in this country, so you can make the connections between actors and work your way up to disrupt the whole chain," said John Carlin, principle associate deputy attorney general at the Justice Department.
 Last month, a cyber criminal group that the U.S. authorities said operates from Russia, penetrated the pipeline operator on the U.S. East Coast, locking its systems and demanding a ransom. The hack caused a shutdown lasting several days, led to a spike in gas prices, panic buying and localized fuel shortages in the southeast.   read more
 Colonial Pipeline decided to pay the hackers who invaded their systems nearly $5 million to regain access, the company said.
 The DOJ guidance specifically refers to Colonial as an example of the "growing threat that ransomware and digital extortion pose to the nation."
 "To ensure we can make necessary connections across national and global cases and investigations, and to allow us to develop a comprehensive picture of the national and economic security threats we face, we must enhance and centralize our internal tracking," said the guidance seen by Reuters and previously unreported.
 The Justice Department's decision to push ransomware into this special process illustrates how the issue is being prioritized, U.S. officials said.
 "We've used this model around terrorism before but never with ransomware," said Carlin. The process has typically been reserved for a short list of topics, including national security cases, legal experts said.
 In practice, it means that investigators in U.S. attorney's offices handling ransomware attacks will be expected to share both updated case details and active technical information with leaders in Washington.
 The guidance also asks the offices to look at and include other investigations focused on the larger cybercrime ecosystem.
 According to the guidance, the list of investigations that now require central notification include cases involving: counter anti-virus services, illicit online forums or marketplaces, cryptocurrency exchanges, bulletproof hosting services, botnets and online money laundering services.
 Bulletproof hosting services refer to opaque internet infrastructure registration services which help cyber criminals to anonymously conduct intrusions.
 A botnet is a group of compromised internet-connected devices that can be manipulated to cause digital havoc. Hackers build, buy and rent out botnets in order to conduct cyber crimes ranging from advertising fraud to large cyberattacks.
 "We really want to make sure prosecutors and criminal investigators report and are tracking ... cryptocurrency exchanges, illicit online forums or marketplaces where people are selling hacking tools, network access credentials - going after the botnets that serve multiple purposes," said Carlin.
 Mark Califano, a former U.S. attorney and cybercrime expert, said the “heightened reporting could allow DOJ to more effectively deploy resources” and to “identify common exploits” used by cybercriminals.

您好，欢迎日常紧缩2021年6月3日。如果您是创始人或早期员工或投资者，今天的TechCrunch前面有很好的新闻：扰乱议程的开始是活的！对于对初创公司的任何人以及他们的成长方式，它将成为一个展示的地狱。到时候那里见！ - 亚历克斯
  United Gues Boom：今天的新闻爆发了美国联合航空公司已同意从繁荣中购买15个超音速喷气机，这是一个专注于建造它们的创业公司。对于繁荣而言，这笔交易是一个很大的事情，物质市场对其产品的需求的证据。据Crunchbase表示，鉴于一般的飞机成本一般，为公司向其投资者展示了一系列大量的预订，这些投资者展示了已经耕种近四十五亿美元的投资者。
 Twitter是蓝色的：没有，社交媒体公司并不悲伤。恰恰相反。相反，Twitter的订阅服务蓝色每月将居住在两个市场中。这是一个非常公开的对Twitter希望 - 我们假设 - 将成为我们无法停止推特的人的全球可用的订阅选项。
 妇女的健康仍然是一个营养业的初创公司：TechCrunch的Natasha Mascarenhas今天挖掘了博客的激素健康世界，询问为什么巨大的市场上没有独角兽。这是一个很好的阅读。
  我们将今天的初创公司和风险投资新闻分为两个桶。第一个包括早期回合，后者在提升的投资有点成熟。
  印度的早期市场加速：曼思辛格报告的TechCrunch认为，这是一系列印度创业公司正在提高资金的过程中。他在他的作品上打破了一件新闻的新闻，而不仅仅是强调全球风险市场的活跃程度，而且追踪所有活动的难度如何。
 简化提高了2.2米，支持营销创意：预计营销人员将产生大量内容。简化正在进行帆布，巨大的市场需求单一的需求。现在它是由Craft Ventures支持的。 
Ganaz筹集了700万美元的帮助，农业工人获得报酬：并非每家科技公司都必须迎合科技精英或富人。 Ganaz正在投注其业务 - 专注于我们所谓的改变“如何与现代劳动力堆栈获得薪水和银行账户的人员如何获得支付和汇款” - 将成为一个受欢迎的人。鉴于农业部门的巨大是多么巨大，其赌注有所了解。
  龚筹集了250万美元的销售自动化：龚的快速增长和最新资金是今天早上我柱的一部分，因为他们证明是有多有趣。简而言之，自去年8月以来，销售自动化公司的估值大致增加了70亿美元。如何？通过去年增长超过2倍。

 Leolabs筹集了65米，让卫星互相击中：随着Spacex将蒲式耳的互联网卫星送入太空，近地轨道中拥挤的问题只会获得贴铃。 Leolabs正在投注，使昂贵的空间技术击中其他空间技术，甚至是太空垃圾，将成为一个增长行业。

 虽然团队收到了优惠，但联合创始人亨利·夏皮罗表示，少数物以言，这些物化的选择不佳，部分是因为回收仍然是预先产品。
 所以一个夏天的早晨，我的联合创始人和我坐在他的车库里 - 我们在过去的一年里一直在原型，投球和迭代 - 并且意识到尽管如此，我们必须完全走开对我们的筹款战略进行全面重置。 
在一个额外的攻击的旅游帖子中，Shapiro分享了他从拥抱失败的知识，并提供了三个结论“每个创始人都应该在他们决定出去和投资者之前考虑。”

note_en:
Hello and welcome to Daily Crunch for June 3, 2021. If you are a startup founder or early employee or investor, there’s good news on the TechCrunch front today: The  start of the Disrupt agenda is live! It’s going to be one hell of a show for anyone interested in startups  and how they grow. See you there! —  Alex
  United goes Boom : News broke today that United Airlines has agreed to purchase 15 supersonic jets from Boom, a startup focused on building them. For Boom, the deal is a big happening, evidence of material market demand for its products. And, given how much planes cost in general, a huge set of bookings for the company to show to its investors that have plowed nearly a quarter billion dollars into the company, according to Crunchbase.
 Twitter is Blue : No, the social media company isn’t sad. Quite the opposite. Instead, Twitter’s subscription service Blue is going live in two markets for a few dollars per month. It’s something of a very public test of what Twitter hopes — we presume — will be a globally available subscription option for those of us who can’t stop tweeting.
 Women’s health remains an underinvested startup niche : TechCrunch’s Natasha Mascarenhas dug into the world of hormonal health for the blog today, asking why there aren’t unicorns in the huge market. It’s a great read.
  We’re dividing up today’s startup and venture capital news into two buckets. The first comprises early-stage rounds, and the latter investments in upstarts that are a bit more mature.
  India’s early-stage market accelerates : Manish Singh reports for TechCrunch that a host of Indian startups are in the process of raising money. He broke an ocean of news in his piece on the matter, not only underscoring how active the global venture market is, but just how hard it can be to keep track of all the activity.
 Simplified raises $2.2M to support marketing creative : Marketers are expected to generate lots of content. Simplified is taking on Canva and that huge market need in a single go. And now it’s backed by Craft Ventures.
 Ganaz raises $7M to help agricultural workers get paid : Not every tech company has to cater to the tech elite or the wealthy. Ganaz is betting that its business — focused on what we described as changing “how people with little documentation and no bank account get paid and send money with a modern workforce stack” — is going to be a hit. Given how huge the agricultural sector is, its wager makes some sense.
  Gong raises $250M for sales automation : Gong’s rapid growth and latest funding was part of  my column this morning because of how interesting they proved to be. In short, the sales automation company has roughly tripled its valuation to more than $7 billion since last August. How? By growing by more than 2x in the last year.
 Realtime Robotics raises $31M for real-time robotics: Boston’s startup scene is more than biotech, it should be clear by now. Realtime Robotics is one such Beantown startup that isn’t building new drugs. Instead, Brian Heater reports, it’s building robot software to “help companies deploy systems with limited programming, offering adaptable controls that work for multiple systems at once.”
 LeoLabs raises $65M to keep satellites from hitting each other: As SpaceX sends bushels of internet satellites into space, the issue of crowding in near-Earth orbit will only get stickier. LeoLabs is betting that keeping expensive space tech from hitting other space tech, or even space trash, is going to be a growth industry.
  Two years ago, founders of calendar-assistant platform Reclaim were looking for a “mango” seed round — a boodle of cash large enough to help them transition from the prototype phase to staffing up for a public launch.
 Although the team received offers, co-founder Henry Shapiro says the few that materialized were poor options, partially because Reclaim was still pre-product.
 So one summer morning, my co-founder and I sat down in his garage — where we’d been prototyping, pitching and iterating for the past year — and realized that as hard as it was, we would have to walk away entirely and do a full reset on our fundraising strategy.
 In a guest post for Extra Crunch, Shapiro shares what he learned from embracing failure and offers three conclusions “every founder should consider before they decide to go out and pitch investors.”
  (Extra Crunch is our membership program, which helps founders and startup teams get ahead.   You can sign up here .)
  Big Tech was busy yet again today, with news from Waymo, Twitter and Blackstone. We also have to talk about the law.
 You can now hail Waymo taxis in Google Maps : Vertical integration, baby! It’s a jam if you are a platform company that makes self-driving cars, operates a taxi service, and also publishes what I presume is the most popular mapping software in the world.
  In related apologies: Waymo is not made up of cowards, but merely businesspeople who should invest more of their testing budget in Providence, Rhode Island.
 Twitter wants to hear you talk : Twitter is bringing its Spaces product more front-and-center in its mobile experience. Sure, all you use Twitter for today is tweets, but Big Tweet will soon want to send your newsletters, host your chats, and, well, distribute your Fleets as well.
 A court case draws limits around a controversial American hacking law: Per TechCrunch, the U.S. Supreme Court “ruled that a police officer who searched a license plate database for an acquaintance in exchange for cash did not violate U.S. hacking laws” in a “landmark ruling [that] concludes a long-running case that clarifies the controversial Computer Fraud and Abuse Act, or CFAA.”
 In terms of legal news and tech, it’s nice to have some good news.
 And, finally,   Blackstone is buying IDG : While your humble TechCrunchers are somewhat sensitive to the idea of private equity buying media properties, the Blackstone-IDG deal is yet another example of the trend.

 作为枢转的创始人，他们在2016年回到Saas，我侧重于缩放我的公司（最近穿越145,000名客户），并在该过程中学到了一些关于制作标记的措施。以下是SaaS启动寿命的各个阶段的差异化的一些建议。
  差异是至关重要的，因为它是吸引客户的唯一方法之一。客户可以帮助为从产品路线图奠定基础。
 您越多了解您的目标客户的痛点与当前的解决方案，它更容易脱颖而出。抓住每个机会了解你旨在服务的人，以及他们想要解决哪些问题。关于特定部门的分析师报告可能是有用的，但没有比希望支付解决方案的人员更好的信息来源。
 SaaS空间成功的关键正在解决真正的问题。例如，拍摄DocaSign  - 该公司找到了一种简单而优雅地解决其软件用户的利基问题的方法。这听起来很容易，但实际上，这意味着花费时间听取客户并相应地定制产品。
note_en:
To scale sustainably, grow its customer base and mature to the point of an exit, a SaaS startup needs to stand apart from the herd at every phase of development. Failure to do so means a poor outcome for founders and investors.
 As a founder who pivoted from on-premise to SaaS back in 2016, I have focused on scaling my company ( most recently crossing 145,000 customers) and in the process, learned quite a bit about making a mark. Here is some advice on differentiation at the various stages in the life of a SaaS startup.
  Differentiation is crucial early on, because it’s one of the only ways to attract customers. Customers can help lay the groundwork for everything from your product roadmap to pricing.
 The more you know about your target customers’ pain points with current solutions, the easier it will be to stand out. Take every opportunity to learn about the people you are aiming to serve, and which problems they want to solve the most. Analyst reports about specific sectors may be useful, but there is no better source of information than the people who, hopefully, will pay to use your solution.
 The key to success in the SaaS space is solving real problems. Take DocuSign, for example — the company found a way to simply and elegantly solve a niche problem for users with its software. This is something that sounds easy, but in reality, it means spending hours listening to the customer and tailoring your product accordingly.

 “我想一段时间，我们几乎完成了”梦想领域“的分析在哪里，”如果我们建造它，他们会来，只看iPhone，＆＃39;“ NAT偷偷困扰着，Aurora的安全负责人在最近与TechCrunch采访中表示。 “我们始终将其与这些其他消费品进行比较，我并不是如此，实际上我们如何赢得美国每一个社区中消费者的心灵和思想。”
  留下以前领导了Uber ATG的安全团队，并曾经在美国交通系中监督自动化车辆的发展，这一目标是无用的车辆 - 无论是罗布西斯穿梭的人或卡车拖运货物 - 广泛采用。他说，这不可能发生，而不能衡量并展示该技术是安全的。他指出，公众信任是他对AV行业所看到的两个最大威胁之一。
  “如果我们担心的是，那些接触到[AVS]的少数人我们将永远不会看到这项技术的好处和广泛的规模，彻底的变化以及我们生活中可以有益的影响， “ 他说。 “我们必须在那里做很多[获得公众信托]。留言补充说，获得公众信任应与政府协会进行。
 “我认为太久了，”你，行业，你解决了它。你正在建造这个东西，＆＃39;“他说。 “我真的认为这是一个合作关系。当然，我们正在建立技术，我们有巨大的责任，而且政府也有一个巨大，巨大的作用，并帮助我们在船上获得公众。“
 安全咨询委员会的成员包括美国总统智能交通协会，戴夫·卡特，戴夫卡豪，前任首席飞行员在波音和维多利亚Chibuogu Nneji，领先的工程师和边缘案例研究中的领先工程师和创新战略家。其他成员包括Biologue总裁Jeff Runge，他也是国家公路交通安全管理局，Adrian Lund，Hitch42，LLC管理员和前总统的公路安全和GHS航空集团首席执行官George Snyder的前总统。
 据说，已经开会的委员会由“不要生活和呼吸技术”的人组成。 
最重要的是，对于Aurora和其余的行业来说，解决了“足够安全的安全性的迫在眉睫的问题”。一项公制，已经采用，越来越批评，正在比较旅行的车里和车辆数英里的每一辆“脱离”，这是一个行业行业术语，这意味着人类安全运营商已经从驾驶车辆的计算机上接管。
 “我们一直很漂亮，这不是一个真正的公制，因为你可以在停车场开车并产生一些互动，这与你在一个城市开车 - 而且顺便说一下，这是一个如果你在高速公路上驾驶，那么很多不同，“偷偷偷偷解释说。
 Aurora是自动化车辆安全财团（AVSC）的一部分，包括戴姆勒，福特，通用汽车，本田，Lyft，动机，SAE和丰田，正在努力提出更好的安全指标。新的极光安全咨询委员会没有直接在AVSC项目上工作，但它提供了可以帮助这项工作的一般指导。
 虽然仍有更多的工作来验证这些新的指标，但该小组确实有少数人认为它思考很有希望，偷偷说。
note_en:
Aurora said Thursday it has tapped experts in aviation safety, insurance, medicine and automotive safety — all people from outside of the niche AV industry — to provide an outside perspective on the company’s overall approach to safety, to look for gaps in its system and advise on the best ways to share its progress and record with regulators and the public. The advisory group is designed to augment Aurora’s existing safety efforts, which includes on-road testing and development.
 “I think for a while we’ve almost done the ‘Field of Dreams’ analysis where it’s like, ‘well if we build it they will come, just look at iPhones,'” Nat Beuse, Aurora’s head of safety said in a recent interview with TechCrunch. “We are always comparing it to these other consumer products, and I’m not so sure that is actually how we win over the hearts and minds of consumers in every single community in the United States.”
  Beuse, who previously led the safety team at Uber ATG and once oversaw automated-vehicle developments at the U.S. Department of Transportation, said the goal is for driverless vehicles — whether that’s robotaxis shuttling people or trucks hauling freight — to be adopted broadly. That can’t happen, he said, without being able to measure and show the public that the technology is safe. He noted that public trust is one of the two biggest threats he sees to the AV industry.
  “If all we worry about is a small number of people who get exposed to [AVs] we will never see the benefits of this technology and the broad scale, sweeping changes and the impact that it can have on our lives in a beneficial way,” he said. “We have to do a lot more there [gaining public trust]. Beuse added that gaining public trust should be done in concert with the government.
 “I think for too long it’s been, ‘You, industry, you solve it. You’re building this stuff,'” he said. “And I really think it’s a partnership. Of course, we’re building the tech, we have a huge responsibility, but also the government has a huge, huge role to play and helping us kind of get the public on board.”
 The members of the  safety advisory board include Intelligent Transportation Society of America President and CEO Shailen Bhatt, Dave Carbaugh, the former chief pilot for flight-operations safety at Boeing and Victoria Chibuogu Nneji, the lead engineer and innovation strategist at Edge Case Research. Other members include Biologue President Jeff Runge, who is also a former administrator of the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration, Adrian Lund, managing member of HITCH42, LLC and former president of the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety and GHS Aviation Group CEO George Snyder.
 The committee, which has already been meeting, is comprised of people who “don’t live and breathe the tech,” Beuse said.
 Most importantly, for Aurora and the rest of the industry, is addressing the looming question of ‘how safe is safe enough?’ when it comes to driverless vehicles. One metric that has been adopted, and increasingly criticized, is comparing vehicle miles traveled and vehicle miles per “disengagement,” an industry jargon term that means a human safety operator has taken over from the computer driving the vehicle.
 “We’ve been pretty adamant that that’s not a real metric because you can drive around in a parking lot and generate some interactions and that’s a whole lot different than if you’re driving in a city — and oh by the way, that’s a whole lot different if you’re driving on the highway,” Beuse explained.
 Aurora is part of the  Automated Vehicle Safety Consortium (AVSC), which includes Daimler, Ford, GM, Honda, Lyft, Motional, SAE and Toyota, that is working to come up with better safety metrics. The new Aurora safety advisory board isn’t working directly on the AVSC project, however it is providing general guidance that could help in this effort.
 While there is still more work to be done to validate these new metrics, the group does have a handful that it thinks are pretty promising, Beuse said.
title: 极光带来了外人，促进了安全努力，公共信任无人驾驶车辆
title_en: Aurora brings in outsiders to boost safety efforts, public trust of driverless vehicles

MIAMI (CBSMiami) – On Wednesday, crews were putting the final touches on the  Bitcoin 2021 Convention, the world’s largest-ever crypto-currency conference to be held this weekend at the Mana Convention Center in Wynwood.
 “We could not be happier. Happier that organizers of the  Bitcoin 2021 Conference have chosen the city of Miami”, said Miami Mayor Francis Suarez who held a virtual international crypto conference Wednesday morning.
 READ MORE:  Florida COVID Hospitalizations At Lowest Point In A Year During the virtual conference, Mayor Suarez alluded to his 4-word tweet from months ago, which grabbed the attention of the crypto industry.
 On camera, Suarez stood up showing a t-shirt, “How can I help?” Suarez said, “In the past 6 months since that tweet, we have seen an unprecedented amount of companies, technologists, and investors choose to move Miami.”
    “Miami is becoming the new capital of capital. It’s becoming an innovative hub. It’s becoming a tier 1 city of venture capital and innovation,” said Brock Pierce. “A lot of the best and the brightest from Silicon Valley and New York have moved their businesses and their firms here.”
 And Brock Pierce should know. He’s the chairman of Bitcoin Foundation who has “mined” his way through the crypto world. Oh and by the way, he’s a billionaire.
 “Mayor Suarez has done an incredible job of saying ‘come here, Miami is the place.’ As a result of it, this has become the most important city in the United States,” Pierce says.
 READ MORE:  13 Internal Applicants Looking To Fill Broward Schools Superintendent Robert Runcie's Shoes  The quality of life is unparalleled. The taxes are great. But for many, bitcoin or cryptocurrency remains an enigma.
  Blake Harris, of Blake Harris Law, who specializes in cryptocurrency explains it’s a digital currency recognized worldwide that can be transferred electronically — and no, it’s not like PayPal.
 “PayPal is not a currency,” said Harris. PayPal is simply a transfer of fiat currency, whereas bitcoin or athereum are its own kind of currency.”
 Crypto is like gold, it can be invested. However, it can’t be manipulated or counterfeited and it’s immune to inflation.
 Harris said, “It’s considered a capital asset. It’s taxed the same way any long-term or short-term asset is taxed. If you hold it for less than a year it’s subject to short-term capital gains. If you hold it longer than a year, it’s subject to long-term capital gains.”
 The currency has been used by terrorist groups such as Hamas, according to the Wall Street Journal.
  MORE NEWS:  Family Of Victim Desmond Owens Ask For Community’s Help After NW Miami Banquet Hall Mass Shooting “It’s the beauty and the risk associated with it. It does not require the approval of a bank. It does not require the approval of a government. And so with that, the government and the banks don’t like what will happen.”

 由于Airtags没有任何方式直接附加到您的东西，因此实际成本高于跟踪器本身的价格，除非您计划只是将一个松散扔进袋子或口袋。苹果的关键环和循环所有成本至少与单一的空中信料一样多，有效地加倍价格（更不用说300美元到450美元的Hermès型号，我们只相信我们）。显然，您可以通过从第三方配件制造商购买相当省略。我们将很快测试一些持有人，并在本指南的更新中包括我们的一些最爱。
 瓷砖伙伴有很长的范围，是非常响亮的，并让您更好地找到除其他非Apple跟踪器的丢失的东西。
 瓷砖必需品在一个实惠的包装中设置捆绑瓷砖配合，瓷砖纤薄和两个瓷砖贴纸。 
Airtags不适用于Android手机，但这并不意味着非Apple人们没有良好的跟踪器选项。蓝牙跟踪器的瓷砖系列，包括瓷砖配合（2020），瓷砖修身（2020）和瓷砖贴纸（2020）（也可以在瓷砖必需品捆绑包装中装配在一起），是下一个最受欢迎的跟踪器，所以他们有第二大人群发现网络。他们还提供令人印象深刻的范围和报警量，它们的尺寸和形状以更方便的方式：您可以在钥匙扣上放置瓷砖伴侣而不购买额外的持有人，将瓷砖纤细滑入您的钱包，或将瓷砖贴纸贴在任何地方平坦的表面。
 如果您的瓷砖超出蓝牙范围，您可以在最后一次手机与其接触时查看显示其位置的地图。如果您的手机不再在那个位置 - 或者您担心它不会 - 您可以将该项目标记为丢失，这会调用Tile的人群查找功能。瓷砖告诉我们，它已售出超过3500万个跟踪器。这并不意味着所有这些都是活跃的，大部分可能不是，因为早期瓷砖跟踪器没有可更换电池，这意味着整个设备必须每年更换。因此，构成其人群发现网络的瓷砖单元总数是苹果的小数。根据公司的数据，每天发现600万物品，并且那些标记为丢失的人，瓷砖表示90％。
 Airtags只有一个圆形，纽扣的设计，但瓷砖线提供更多选项。瓷砖配合是最通用的设计，左上角的开口，可让您附上钥匙扣，挂绳或类似的系绳。这是一个圆角正方形，约为1.38英寸，比火柴盒厚0.24英寸。瓷砖纤细是瓷砖设计用于钱包的硬件;它的形状是信用卡，2.4毫米，它与其中三个一样厚。瓷砖贴纸大致堆叠四个季度的尺寸，小圆形跟踪器与任何想要跟踪的粘合剂都粘贴。这使得贴纸是一个没有钥匙圈或系绳的任何东西的好选择，因为这个跟踪器如此小，你可以将其附加到谨慎的位置。
  在我们的距离测试中，瓷砖伴侣没有从蓝牙跟踪器看到的最佳范围，但它的结果令人印象深刻，我们认为大多数人需要在瓷砖专业人员上花费额外的钱。在与我们的iPhone失败之前，我们能够平均地距离约152英尺。这少于200英尺，瓷砖广告，但它仍然是一个令人印象深刻的数字。虽然瓷砖专业人士优于这个数字，但是150英尺比你可能认为它是 - 大约一个半倍 - 大约一个和半倍 - 大多数家庭或办公室都有很多津贴。瓷砖纤细保持连接至144.4英尺，瓷砖贴纸在我们的测试中具有120.2英尺的范围。虽然瓷砖追踪器并不像空中拖车那样精确，但是在附近的距离大于您与Apple的追踪者获得的30脚更多。
 我们以超过104 dB的瓷砖伴侣测量啁啾声，在99.5 dB的纤细的薄膜，以及103.9 dB的贴纸上的那些肯定响亮，可以在办公室或公寓的另一侧听到。
 瓷砖配合在用户可更换的CR1632锂电池上运行，您可以通过背面的滑动门轻松访问。瓷砖表示电池将在需要更换之前持续一年;这些电池很容易获得1美元的5美元。这是我们预期的电池寿命大致相同。 （如果通过默认程序升级较旧的瓦片，您将收到一个新版本。）不幸的是，瓷砖纤细和瓷砖贴纸都没有使用可更换电池，但瓷砖表示他们的电池将持续三年。
 因为电池门可能被重新安装，所以瓦片说它无法称之为防水。但该公司告诉我们，仍然认为跟踪器是防水的，这意味着将其放在水坑里或在雨中留下它不应该杀死它。根据瓷砖，测试单元在烘干机中存活了1,000个循环，因此您应该期望硬件比电池更长。苗条和伴侣均均额定IPX7，因此它们可以承受浸入水中深处的水中。 
瓷砖提供瓷砖溢价，可选，每月3美元（或每年30美元）订阅服务，这些服务涵盖您拥有的多块瓷砖。该服务包括每年运送给您的免费电池更换，从一年到三，以及基于SMS的支持。在软件方面，提供30天的位置历史记录，无限制地分享您的瓦片追踪器的位置，以及智能警报，即使您在没有任何瓷砖的情况下离开家时，可以让您知道。 （最后一个功能基于输入您的家庭地址，并在我们的测试中，我们可以在收到警报之前获得三分之一的一英里，这不是超级方便的警报。）Apple不提供任何这些功能Airtag。每年100美元，瓷砖还提供高级保护，其特点加上丢失物品的每年偿还1000美元。瓷砖溢价是大多数人的不必要的费用，但如果您正在跟踪有价值的东西，高级保护可能值得投资。
 设置任何瓷砖很简单：而不是必须进入手机的设置菜单以添加图块，图块应用程序（Android，iOS）处理所有内容。您只需启动应用程序，点击一个按钮添加新图块，并在出现提示时牢牢按下Tile的中心按钮。如果您有iPhone，Apple的Airtag Setup可能会更加自动，但两个流程都快速简单。
 在应用程序中，您可以为每个瓷砖命名，无论您喜欢什么并添加照片;例如，如果在背包中有一个瓷砖配合，则可以添加那个特定瓦片的背包照片。您可以在帐户列表或地图模式下查看您的设备 - 所有图块，配对手机以及运行图块应用程序的任何其他手机和平板电脑。您可以使用手机搭配无限数量的瓷砖，但只能通过蓝牙与Android手机携带四块瓷砖。 （无限数量的瓷砖可以一次与Apple设备通信。）
 瓷砖的精确度较小，而不是短距离的空间，因为它们使用标准形式的蓝牙，没有超宽带技术层，使得查找我的应用程序如此准确。在2021年4月的普通法律顾问达卢颁发的国会议长（PDF）中，瓷砖表示，它已开发出一种使用超宽带技术的产品，但苹果正在防止与iPhone兼容。
 突出隐私和安全之间的平衡，其中包含追踪您的东西是困难的，并且跟踪您所在地的设备呈现了许多问题。除了担心追踪者制造商与您的位置信息中的内容担忧，还有故意滥用和滥用的潜在滥用行为者。苹果和瓷砖在两个前面都会采取不同的粘性。
 Apple表示，它设计了凭借隐私的Airtag。因为位置数据是加密端到端的，因为除了你甚至是苹果的没有一个，因为甚至是Apple  - 都可以看到你的Airtag是哪里。回到苹果公司制作了Airtag宣布并发布了详细信息时，该公司概述了它试图防止空中空间能够实现某些类型的滥用的方式。例如，如果有人试图通过将空气抛在您的包中偷偷摸摸地追踪您，iPhone（如果您使用一个）会注意到在您周围的外部跟踪器并向您发送警报。我们的测试尚未令人穷的令人穷，让我们确保在获得警报之前需要多久。如果您没有iPhone，大胆的火球的John Gruber报告称，从其所有者分开的空中信料将在三天后开始令人振奋。华盛顿邮政（订阅所需）广泛测试了ICHONES的Airtag隐私功能，得出结论，Apple的特征添加以限制追踪可能不会阻止不良行为，就像一个人可能希望。威胁人员能够跟踪您的位置，三天是很长一段时间。此外，如果您的秘密空中的物品被盗而不是丢失，小偷可能会得到为期三天的警报，从理论上可以禁用跟踪或扔掉空气拖放。瓦片没有类似的系统，所以你不会知道附近的瓦片，除非你物理地找到它。
 瓦片代表告诉我们，该公司以四种方式使用手机的位置数据：确定您与您的东西相关的位置，定期确定每个跟踪器的位置，如果您在别人丢失的追踪器的范围内，则指出您的位置。 （如果您联系瓷砖的支持人员），请通过IP地址确定计算机或其他设备的近似位置。我们不认为这些用途是对您购买以找到您和您的东西的设备的任何一种使用，并且瓦片告诉我们，它不会“在我们的产品或服务中使用收入，以便为第三方提供给予第三方的服务获得货币收益的某些信息。“ 
我们通过每个公司的隐私政策梳理，并致于澄清关于我们关于这些类型的跟踪人员最关键的问题。这是他们的比较方式：
 Chipolo的一个位置使用Apple的找到我的网络，这意味着它就像某人的iPhone一样可能会匿名找到你丢失的跟踪器，因为它是一个空中特典。但是一个地方只有一个优势在Airtag-An keychain洞中，作为其设计的一部分 - 以及许多缺点。它没有精确的查找能力，它不像元素一样抵抗，它的身体更大。与Chipolo一（2020）（身体上相同但使用Chipolo的众筹网络而不是Apple），一个点不能用来触发手机上的警报，或者如果您留下了一些东西，请告诉您。没有理由选择空中信料的一个地方，特别是因为它们的成本大致相同。
 Tile Pro（2020）是瓷砖跟踪器的更昂贵且稍大的瓷砖追踪版本，具有更长的范围。瓷砖表示，该模型将保持高达400英尺，或40英尺长度超过足球场的长度。在我们的测试中，Tile Pro仍然连接在我们办公室的整个190英尺长度上并保持联系。虽然更长的范围更好，但我们得出结论，在家庭或办公室周围的任何情景中，瓷砖伴侣就足够了。如果您的房子超过400英尺，可以为瓷砖专业人员摆动额外的10美元。
 轨道提供许多跟踪器样式，包括轨道眼镜跟踪器，适合眼镜或太阳镜，以及轨道卡，卡式追踪器，甚至比瓷砖纤细更薄。如果您在那些特定的设计中需要跟踪，他们是有趣的，但遗憾的是，轨道网络对于我们建议选择轨道的轨道网络太有限。轶事，我失去了一副太阳镜，在繁忙和科技纽约市的轨道眼镜追踪器配备。尽管将它们标记为应用程序中丢失，但希望轨道用户通过并找到它们，我从未收到过另一个轨道用户发现太阳镜的通知。此外，当轨道卡在电池电量上运行低时，我收到了没有通知，这可能导致丢失的钱包无法找到它。
 Trackr的像素明显小于瓷砖配合。在直径上测量大约一英寸，它是关于现代美元硬币的大小，它与三个季度的堆叠一样厚。在2017年6月的头部测试中，Trackr像素在断开之前平均45.1英尺的距离，比任何瓦片模型的结果短都短。较短的范围和TrackR的较小网络使我们能够推荐此模型。
 立方体提供三个追踪器，立方体，立方体专业人员和立方体阴影的三个设计，其中一个是一个纤薄的折叠尺寸。虽然立方体阴影承诺与瓷砖苗条相同的范围，但立方体和立方体专业人员分别提供瓷砖伴侣和瓷砖专业人士的一半，而整个立方体家族具有比瓷砖的追踪者更小的人群查找网络。
 尼克家伙是一名高级工作人员，覆盖着Wirecutter的苹果和配件。自2011年以来，他一直在审查iPhone，iPad和相关技术 - 在他测试了第1,000案之前停止计数。他不可能在精神上目录无论他看到任何案例是不可能的。他曾经有明亮的想法来建造和烧毁一个房间来测试防火保险箱。 
蜂窝GPS跟踪器允许您在您的内容上保持实时选项卡。 智能制品跟踪器是准确的，电池寿命长，而且昂贵。  哨子3是大多数宠物所有者最好的GPS宠物跟踪器，因为快速，准确的跟踪和远视我们测试的任何型号的最长电池寿命。  蓝牙设备并不总是在一起播放，特别是在群组中。 我们解释为什么他们有时很难连接，以及你可以做些什么。  Jabra Elite 75T蓝牙耳塞听起来很棒，在耳朵里感到舒适，并提供完全无线电缆的便利性。

If you’re lucky, the $30 you spend on a Bluetooth tracker will be wasted money. These tags are helpful only when you need to find your stuff—if you never lose things, they’re just ornamental. But if you’re often scrambling to locate your keys or bag as you’re heading out the door, a Bluetooth tracker can be a lifesaver. iPhone users should choose  Apple’s AirTag, which employs a huge network of Apple devices to passively search for lost goods and enable precision finding features, making it more likely that you’ll recover what you’ve misplaced. If you use an Android phone, go with Tile trackers, including the  Tile Mate (2020). With the second-largest crowd-finding network, Tile is the best option for those who don’t use Apple’s phones.
 AirTags leverage Apple’s huge network of devices to find lost goods, and they are impressively accurate in pinpointing an item’s precise location.
 Bluetooth trackers let you monitor the location of whatever they’re attached to from a phone, tablet, or computer. For iPhone owners,  Apple’s AirTag is an easy top pick. When the tracker is out of range of your phone, it’s designed to work in unison with other Apple devices, which means there’s nearly a billion devices constantly looking for lost items. This vast network makes it more likely that something you drop will be found. When your AirTag is in range of your phone, it also uses technology beyond regular Bluetooth to show you where your lost item is, within inches. No other tracker can compete with the broad scope and precision finding that an AirTag offers.
  The Tile Mate has a long range, is quite loud, and gives you a better chance of finding your lost stuff than other non-Apple trackers.
 The Tile Essentials set bundles the Tile Mate, the Tile Slim, and two Tile Stickers in one affordable package.
 AirTags don’t work with Android phones, but Tile’s trackers, including the  Tile Mate (2020),  Tile Slim (2020), and  Tile Sticker (2020), which are also available in a  bundle, are the best alternative. Tile as a company has become synonymous with trackers in the way Kleenex is with facial tissue. Its trackers offer the second-largest crowd-finding network, as well as impressive range. You can also use a Tile to remotely trigger a lost phone to ring, something you can’t do with AirTags.
 A cellular GPS tracker lets you keep real-time tabs on your stuff. The  SmartThings Tracker is accurate, has a long battery life, and isn’t pricey.
 Over the past few years, we’ve tested more than a dozen Bluetooth trackers. The major players in this category are well known. But to make sure we didn’t miss any, during our research for a previous version of this guide, we turned to Amazon and Google. The latter search turned up some great comparative reviews from  Tom’s Guide,  The Wall Street Journal (subscription required),  Wired, and  Yahoo.
  Network size: Most Bluetooth trackers can utilize other phones that have the tracker’s app installed to silently and privately search for your lost objects—so the more people who use a platform, the better your chances of recovering your lost stuff.
 Precision finding: Once you know the general location of your lost tracker, narrowing down exactly where it’s hiding is the key to finding it. Trackers use a combination of physical speakers and digital apps to help you locate your stuff.
 Size: A tracker that’s too obtrusive to carry around with you or to attach to the thing you want to track is useless. Smaller and lighter is better.
 Range: The longer the range, the farther you can be from the tracker and still be able to find your lost item.
 Volume: If you’re near your tracker but you can’t hear it, that’s no good. Despite having little space for a speaker, some of these can get quite loud!
 Other features: A replaceable battery isn’t absolutely required, but it’s a big plus. We also prefer trackers that can trigger an alarm on your phone. An app that is easy to navigate and updates location information quickly is also important.
 Prior to the 2021 introduction of Apple’s AirTag, the most important test we conducted was to determine each tracker’s potential range—how far away we could move a paired smartphone from the tracker before we lost the Bluetooth connection. In 2019, we tested the range in Wirecutter’s 190-foot-long office in Long Island City, New York. We set each tracker on a shelf, triggered it to ring, and then walked away with our paired iPhone while measuring the distance using a  measuring wheel. Once the tracker’s smartphone app showed the tracker as out of range or we physically hit the wall, we recorded the distance. We repeated this test at least five times per tracker and calculated the average distance for each tracker across the measurements. Because AirTags leverage a much larger network and are more precise once you’re in range, this distance test is still part of our evaluation but not as important.
  Unlike Tile’s trackers and some others, the AirTag doesn’t have any way to ring your phone. So if it’s your iPhone that’s lost, you’ll have to rely on the Find My app on another device to find it.
 There’s no way to share an AirTag with another person because the tracker can be associated with only one Apple ID, even if you use Apple’s Family Sharing plan. If you share a set of keys with a partner or a roommate, for example, only one of you will be able to track them in the Find My app.
 AirTags don’t include a separation alert, meaning you won’t get a notification if you leave your keys behind. Of course, you can use the app to find your lost items once you discover they’re missing, but the system has no proactive method of letting you know you forgot something.
 Because AirTags don’t have any way to directly attach to your stuff, the actual cost is higher than the price of the tracker itself unless you plan on just tossing one loose into a bag or pocket. Apple’s key rings and loops all cost at least as much as a single AirTag, effectively doubling the price (not to mention the  $300 to  $450 Hermès models we are linking to only so that you believe us). Obviously, you can save quite a bit by buying from a third-party accessory maker. We’ll be testing some holders soon and including a few of our favorites in an update to this guide.
 The Tile Mate has a long range, is quite loud, and gives you a better chance of finding your lost stuff than other non-Apple trackers.
 The Tile Essentials set bundles the Tile Mate, the Tile Slim, and two Tile Stickers in one affordable package.
 AirTags don’t work with Android phones, but that doesn’t mean non-Apple folks don’t have a good tracker option. The Tile family of Bluetooth trackers, including the  Tile Mate (2020),  Tile Slim (2020), and  Tile Sticker (2020) (also available packed together in the  Tile Essentials bundle), are the next most popular trackers, so they have the second-largest crowd-finding network. They also offer impressive range and alarm volume, and they are sized and shaped in more convenient ways: You can put a Tile Mate on a keychain without buying an extra holder, slip a Tile Slim into your wallet, or attach a Tile Sticker to any flat surface.
 If your Tile is out of Bluetooth range, you can view a map displaying its location the last time your phone made contact with it. If your phone is no longer at that location—or you’re worried that it won’t be—you can mark the item as lost, which invokes Tile’s crowd-finding feature. Tile tells us it has sold more than 35 million trackers. That doesn’t mean all of them are currently active, and a big portion likely aren’t, as early Tile trackers didn’t have replaceable batteries, which meant the entire device had to be replaced annually. So the total number of Tile units that make up its crowd-finding network is a tiny fraction of Apple’s. According to the company’s figures, however, 6 million items are found daily, and of those marked as lost, Tile says 90% are found.
 AirTags come in only a single round, button-like design, but the Tile line offers more options. The Tile Mate is the most general-purpose design, with an opening in the top-left corner that lets you attach a keychain, lanyard, or similar tether. It’s a round-cornered square that’s about 1.38 inches across and 0.24 inch thick—smaller than a matchbook. The Tile Slim is Tile’s hardware designed for a wallet; it’s in the shape of a credit card, and at 2.4 millimeters, it’s as thick as three of them. The Tile Sticker is roughly the size of four quarters stacked, and the small round tracker sticks with adhesive to whatever you want to track. This makes the Sticker a good option for anything that doesn’t have a key ring or tether, and because this tracker is so small, you can attach it in a discreet spot.
  In our distance test, the Tile Mate didn’t have the best range we’d ever seen from a Bluetooth tracker, but its results were impressive enough that we don’t think most people need to spend the extra money on the  Tile Pro. We were able to get an average of about 152 feet away before losing the connection with our iPhone. That’s less than the 200 feet that Tile advertises, but it’s still an impressive number. Although the Tile Pro outperformed that figure, 150 feet is longer than you might think it is—about one and a half times the length of a basketball court—and it’s plenty of allowance for most homes or offices. The Tile Slim stayed connected until 144.4 feet away, and the Tile Sticker had a 120.2-foot range in our test. Though a Tile tracker is not as precise as an AirTag, that’s a lot more nearby-finding distance than the 30 or so feet you get with Apple’s tracker.
 We measured the chirps from the Tile Mate at just over 104 dB, those of the Slim at 99.5 dB, and those of the Sticker at 103.9 dB—all certainly loud enough to hear on the opposite side of an office or apartment.
 The Tile Mate runs on a user-replaceable CR1632 lithium battery that you can easily access via a slide-off door on the back. Tile says the battery will last a year before it needs to be replaced; these batteries are readily available for less than $1 apiece. That’s roughly the same battery life we expect from an AirTag. (If you upgrade an older Tile through the  reTile program, you’ll receive one of the new versions.) Unfortunately, neither the Tile Slim nor the Tile Sticker uses a replaceable battery, though Tile says their batteries will last for three years.
 Because the battery door could possibly be reinstalled incorrectly, Tile says it can’t call the Tile Mate waterproof. But the company has told us it still considers that tracker to be water resistant, which means that dropping it in a puddle or leaving it out in the rain shouldn’t kill it. According to Tile, test units survived 1,000 cycles in a dryer, so you should expect the hardware to last longer than the battery. The Slim and Mate are both rated IPX7, so they can withstand being submerged in water up to a meter deep.
 Tile offers  Tile Premium, an optional, $3-per-month (or $30-per-year) subscription service that covers as many Tiles as you own. The service includes free battery replacements shipped to you each year, a warranty extension from one year to three, and SMS-based support. On the software side, it offers a 30-day location history, unlimited sharing of your Tile trackers’ locations, and smart alerts that let you know when you’ve left home without any of your Tiles. (The last feature is based on entering your home address, and in our testing, we could get a third of a mile away before receiving the alert, which isn’t super handy.) Apple doesn’t provide any of these features with an AirTag. For $100 a year, Tile also offers Premium Protect, which has the same features plus a $1,000-per-year reimbursement for lost items. Tile Premium is an unnecessary expense for most people, but Premium Protect may be worth the investment if you’re tracking something valuable.
 Setting up any Tile is simple: Instead of having to go into your phone’s Settings menu to add the Tile, the Tile app ( Android,  iOS) handles everything. You just launch the app, tap a button to add a new Tile, and press firmly on the Tile’s center button when prompted. If you have an iPhone, Apple’s AirTag setup may be a little more automatic, but both processes are fast and simple.
 In the app, you can name each Tile whatever you like and add a photo; for example, if you have a Tile Mate in your backpack, you can add a photo of the backpack for that particular Tile. You can view your devices—all Tiles, your paired phone, and any other phones and tablets running the Tile app under your account—in list or map mode. You can pair an unlimited number of Tiles with a phone, but  only four Tiles can be actively communicating with an Android phone, via Bluetooth, at once. (An unlimited number of Tiles can communicate with an Apple device at once.)
 Tiles have less precision than AirTags over short distances because they use a standard form of Bluetooth, without the layer of Ultra Wideband technology that makes the Find My app so accurate. In  oral testimony to Congress (PDF) presented by its general counsel Kirsten Daru in April 2021, Tile said it had developed a product that uses Ultra Wideband technology, but that Apple is preventing compatibility with iPhones.
 Striking a balance between privacy and security with an item meant to track your stuff is difficult, and a device that tracks your location presents a number of concerns. Beyond the worry about what the tracker maker does with your location information, there’s also the potential for intentional misuse and abuse by bad actors. Apple and Tile take different tacks on both fronts.
 Apple says it designed the AirTag with privacy in mind. Because the location data is encrypted end-to-end, no one other than you—not even Apple—can see where your AirTag is. Back when Apple made the AirTag announcement and released the details, the company outlined the ways in which it is trying to prevent AirTags from  enabling certain types of abuse. For example, if someone tries to surreptitiously track you by throwing an AirTag into your bag, your iPhone (if you use one) will notice a foreign tracker following you around and send you an alert. Our testing hasn’t been exhaustive enough yet for us to be sure how it long takes before you get an alert. If you don’t have an iPhone, Daring Fireball’s  John Gruber reports that an AirTag separated from its owner will start beeping after three days.  The Washington Post (subscription required), which extensively tested the AirTag privacy features with iPhones, concluded that the features Apple added to limit stalking may not deter bad behavior as much as one might hope. Three days is a long time for a threatening person to be able to track your location. Further, if your secretly AirTagged item is stolen instead of lost, the thief may get the three-day alert and theoretically could disable the tracking or just throw the AirTag away. Tile doesn’t have a similar system, so you won’t know about a nearby planted Tile unless you physically find it.
 Tile representatives have told us that the company uses your phone’s location data in four ways: determining where you are in relation to your stuff, periodically determining the location of each tracker, noting your location if you’re in range of someone else’s lost tracker, and (if you contact Tile’s support staff) determining the approximate location of your computer or other device by IP address. We don’t think any of these uses is particularly invasive for a device that you’re purchasing to locate you and your stuff, and Tile told us it doesn’t “use revenue generating advertising in our products or services that could give third parties access to certain information for monetary gain.”
 We combed through the privacy policies of each company and reached out with clarifying questions about our most critical concerns regarding these types of trackers. Here’s how they compare:
 Chipolo’s One Spot uses Apple’s Find My network, meaning it’s just as likely someone’s iPhone will anonymously find your lost tracker as it is with an AirTag. But the One Spot only has one advantage over the AirTag—an keychain hole as part of its design—and a number of disadvantages. It doesn’t have the precise finding capabilities, it’s not as resistant to the elements, and it’s physically larger. Unlike the  Chipolo One (2020) (which is physically identical but uses Chipolo’s crowdfinding network instead of Apple’s), the One Spot can’t be used to trigger an alert on your phone, or let you know if you’ve left something behind. There’s no reason to choose the One Spot over the AirTag, especially since they cost about the same.
 The  Tile Pro (2020) is a more expensive and slightly larger version of the Tile tracker, with a longer range. Tile says this model will stay connected for up to 400 feet, or 40 feet longer than the length of a football field. In our tests, the Tile Pro remained connected for the entire, 190-foot length of our office and remained connected. Though a longer range is better, we’ve concluded that in almost any scenario around a home or office, the Tile Mate is sufficient. If your house is longer than 400 feet, you can swing the extra $10 for the Tile Pro.
 Orbit offers a number of tracker styles, including the  Orbit Glasses tracker, which fits on eyeglasses or sunglasses, and the  Orbit Card, a card-style tracker that’s even thinner than the Tile Slim. They’re intriguing if you need tracking in those specific designs, but unfortunately the Orbit network is just too limited for us to recommend choosing Orbit over Tile. Anecdotally, I lost a pair of sunglasses that were outfitted with the Orbit Glasses tracker in busy and tech-heavy New York City. And despite marking them as lost in the app in hopes of an Orbit user passing by and finding them, I never received a notice that the sunglasses were found by another Orbit user. Additionally, I received no notification when the Orbit Card was running low on battery power, which could potentially lead to a lost wallet with no way to find it.
 TrackR’s Pixel is noticeably smaller than the Tile Mate. Measuring just about an inch in diameter, it’s about the size of a modern dollar coin, and it’s as thick as a stack of three quarters. In head-to-head tests in June 2017, the TrackR Pixel averaged a distance of 45.1 feet before disconnecting, shorter than the results for any of the Tile models. That shorter range and TrackR’s smaller network keep us from recommending this model.
 Cube offers three designs of its tracker, the  Cube, the  Cube Pro, and the  Cube Shadow, the last of which is a slim, wallet-able size. Though the Cube Shadow promises the same range as the Tile Slim, the Cube and Cube Pro offer half the range of the Tile Mate and Tile Pro, respectively, and the whole Cube family has a smaller crowd-finding network than Tile’s trackers.
 Nick Guy is a senior staff writer covering Apple and accessories at Wirecutter. He has been reviewing iPhones, iPads, and related tech since 2011—and stopped counting after he tested his 1,000th case. It’s impossible for him not to mentally catalog any case he sees. He once had the bright idea to build and burn down a room to test fireproof safes.
 A cellular GPS tracker lets you keep real-time tabs on your stuff. The  SmartThings Tracker is accurate, has a long battery life, and isn’t pricey.
 The  Whistle 3 is the best GPS pet tracker for most pet owners thanks to quick, accurate tracking and by far the longest battery life of any model we tested.
 Bluetooth devices don’t always play nicely together, especially in groups. We explain why it’s hard for them to connect sometimes, and what you can do about it.
 The  Jabra Elite 75t Bluetooth earbuds sound great, feel comfortable in the ears, and offer the convenience of being completely cable-free.
